title: Chapter 33: The Armor
content: 5:00 PM. Four hours.

The suite at the Plaza Athénée was too quiet. Jane stood in the middle of it, barefoot on the herringbone parquet, and listened to the silence the way a patient listens for a second opinion — hoping it says something different from the first.

It didn't. The silence told her exactly what she already knew: she was alone. Edward had left an hour ago, dispatched to the venue with the grim purposefulness of a general surveying a battlefield before dawn. Last-minute preparations, he'd said. The lighting rig needed adjusting. The seating chart had to be finalized. The models were arriving for hair and makeup. He'd kissed her forehead before he left — a gesture so reflexive it might have been applied to a mannequin — and she'd let him, because that was part of it too. Everything was part of it now.
She turned toward the closet.

Two garments hung on the door, and they could not have been more different. The Thornfield gown faced outward: oatmeal silk, boat neckline, sleeves that fell to the wrist in clean, unbroken lines. It was beautiful the way a matte wall was beautiful — in its refusal to declare itself. Minimalist. Erasing. Edward had approved it personally, had held the silk swatch against her jawline and nodded as though confirming a calculation. It would make her look like what he needed her to look like tonight: the architect's consort. The silent, elegant frame around his masterpiece.
Behind it, hidden in a black garment bag, hung the crimson dress.
Jane unzipped the bag.

The fabric caught the late-afternoon light falling through the tall windows and seemed to drink it. Raw silk — not the polished, domesticated kind, but the kind with a slight nap, a texture that caught on the fingertips like something alive. The color was not the red of roses or rubies or any of the safe, metaphorical reds. It was the red of arterial blood, fresh and unapologetic. The red that said: I am here, and I am not sorry.
She carried it to the bed and laid it out across the white duvet. It spread across the pale fabric like a wound.
*Two weapons*, she thought. One for the world to see. One for the world to remember.
She stripped off the hotel robe and stood for a moment in her underwear, the Paris air from the half-open window cool against her skin. Then she picked up the crimson dress.

She stepped into it one leg at a time, drawing the raw silk up over her thighs, her hips, her waist. The fabric whispered against her skin — friction and softness at once, the way a blade is both edge and steel. She slipped her arms through the cap sleeves and reached behind her for the zipper. It moved smoothly, the metal teeth closing like a seam being sealed, and then she was in it.
She turned to the mirror.

The woman looking back at her was not the woman who had walked into Thornfield fourteen months ago. She was not the archive consultant who had stood in Edward's library and cataloged his silences. She was not the fiancée who had accepted a ring while something in her chest went quiet and still, folding itself into smaller and smaller compartments to survive. The woman in the mirror was none of those versions, and she was all of them. She was the version that existed before Edward — before the château, before the silk, before the careful architecture of a love that was really a cage dressed in couture.
She was just Jane.

The crimson dress fit perfectly. Of course it did — she'd had it altered twice in secret, measurements taken in the back room of a seamstress's shop on Rue de Rivoli where no one knew her name, where no one would remember her face. The bodice structured her without constraining her. The skirt fell to mid-calf in a clean line that moved when she moved, that had weight and presence without apology. She turned slightly, watching the fabric shift, watching the color deepen and lighten as the city's dying sun moved behind the clouds outside.
*This is who I am*, she thought. *This is who I was. This is who I'll be when the dust settles and the château is just an address I used to know.*

She stood there for a long moment, breathing in the quiet, holding the image of herself in red like a photograph she wanted to memorize. Because in a few hours, this woman would have to disappear. Temporarily. Strategically. The way a missile disappears into its silo before launch.
She reached for the oatmeal gown.

The shell was heavier than the crimson dress — not in weight, exactly, but in the way it carried itself. The silk had been treated, pressed, starched into a kind of architectural firmness that held its shape even on the hanger. It was structured the way Edward structured everything: with invisible boning at the waist, with a built-in modesty panel at the neckline, with a zipper that was hidden so perfectly it was nearly invisible.

Jane stepped into it carefully, pulling it up over the crimson dress like slipping into a second skin. The oatmeal silk closed around the red like a door closing, like a hand over a mouth. She reached behind her and found the hidden zipper. It moved with the same silky precision as the first, but this closure felt different. This one was erasure.
She looked in the mirror again.

The color drained. The woman in red vanished, replaced by — something else. Paler. Quieter. Controlled. The oatmeal silk turned her into a study in restraint, a canvas wiped clean, a negative space. She became Edward's Jane. The Jane he'd sculpted, curated, dressed. Beautiful in the way he defined beauty — which was to say, beautiful in the way that made her beauty a reflection of his taste rather than her own.

But underneath — one zipper away, hidden against her skin — the crimson dress was still there. She could feel it. The raw silk pressed close to her body, warm now from her heat, alive against her ribs. No one looking at her would see it. No one would imagine it. And that was the point.
The armor isn't what the world sees. The armor is what you carry underneath.

She turned left, then right, watching herself in the mirror from different angles. The oatmeal shell was seamless. Impeccable. Anyone — Edward, Blanche, the press, the buyers — would see exactly what they expected to see: the designer's muse, dressed in his aesthetic, an extension of his vision. They would see compliance. They would see devotion. They would see her the way he had taught them to see her.

But Jane saw both women now. The one on the surface and the one underneath. They occupied the same body, the same space, the same moment. They were separated by nothing more than a hidden zipper and a few millimeters of treated silk. They coexisted the way a secret coexists with a lie — in the same room, breathing the same air, waiting for the moment when one will outlive the other.

In a few hours, one would step out of the other. Like a butterfly from a chrysalis, if butterflies were forged in fury instead of metamorphosis. If butterflies destroyed the thing that built the cocoon instead of simply leaving it behind.
Jane would not simply leave. Jane would unravel.
She crossed to the vanity where her jewelry was laid out. Earrings first — the simple pearl studs Edward had chosen. Then the sapphire ring.

She picked it up and felt its weight in her palm. The stone was dark, almost black in low light, a deep-sea blue that only revealed itself in direct sun. Edward had given it to her in the garden at Thornfield, on a June evening that smelled of jasmine and lies. *It belonged to my mother*, he'd said, which she'd later learned was not true — it had belonged to a supplier, part of a closing-out deal, worth perhaps a third of what he'd implied. But the size was real enough, and the fit had been suspiciously perfect.

She'd learned why later. Grace had told her, with the detached precision of a woman reading a medication label: *He had your ring size measured while you slept. One of the staff slipped a sizing band on your finger after you'd taken the sleeping pill.*

The ring that measured her while she slept. She wore it now as she'd worn it every day since the garden — on the fourth finger of her left hand, where fiancées wear promises. The cold sapphire pressed against her skin, and she felt the chill of it move through her like a warning.

She would remove it tonight. After the show. After the world saw what she saw when she looked in the mirror — both women at once, the erased and the indelible. After the zipper came down and the crimson stepped out and the cameras caught what Edward Rochester had never anticipated. Then she would take the ring off and leave it somewhere — on a table, in a room, in a life that no longer belonged to her.

But for now, she wore it. Because even his ring was part of the performance. Every piece of his architecture — the dress, the ring, the narrative — was a prop, and she was the one who would decide when the play was over.
A knock at the door.

Jane's hand dropped from the ring. She crossed the room and checked the peephole before opening — a habit she'd developed somewhere between the sleeping pills and the surveillance files, a habit that had become instinct.
Adèle stood in the hallway.

She was dressed for the show: a simple black dress that fell just above the knee, flat shoes, her dark hair pulled back in a low ponytail. No jewelry except for small gold studs in her ears. The look was her own choice — Jane could tell because it didn't look like anyone's idea of what a sixteen-year-old should wear to Paris Fashion Week. It looked like what Adèle wanted to wear. It looked like herself.
Her eyes were wide. Nervous. Brave.
"Are you ready?" she asked.

Jane looked at her — this girl who had chosen to see when it would have been easier to look away. This girl who had smuggled a hard drive in the hollowed-out pages of a Baudelaire edition because a woman she barely knew had asked her to, because something in the asking had resonated with something in the being-asked. Adèle had seen the shape of her father's darkness the way children see the shapes in dark rooms — not because they're more imaginative, but because they're more honest about being afraid.
"No," Jane said. Honestly. Completely.
Adèle blinked.

"That's not the same as not doing it," Jane added. She held the girl's gaze and saw the recognition pass through her — the understanding that readiness was a luxury that people like them had never been afforded, and that the absence of it was not disqualification but confirmation.

Adèle stepped forward and reached up. Her fingers found a strand of Jane's hair that had escaped the careful arrangement and tucked it back, smoothing it behind her ear. A small gesture. A human one. The kind of gesture a mother might make, or a sister, or a friend — the categories didn't matter, only the touch.
"He's not going to know what hit him," Adèle said. The words were quiet, but they carried the weight of a girl who had grown up inside a house of silences and emerged with her voice intact.

Jane felt something move in her chest — not warmth exactly, but something adjacent to it. Something hard and bright and unbreakable. Not love, or not only love. Solidarity. The recognition of one survivor for another.
"Let's go," she said.

They left the suite together. Down the hallway with its Damask wallpaper and its discreet lighting, past doors behind which other guests were living other lives, unaware that the woman in the oatmeal dress was carrying a revolution beneath her ribs. The elevator arrived with a soft chime, and they stepped inside. Adèle pressed the button for the lobby.
The doors closed.

In the mirrored wall of the elevator, Jane caught her reflection again: pale, composed, the model of restraint. But she could feel the crimson underneath — against her skin, against her spine, against the pulse point at the small of her back where the raw silk pressed closest. The two garments moved differently when she walked. The oatmeal was rigid, correct. The crimson shifted with her, fluid and alive, like a second heartbeat.

The lobby was busy with the pre-show bustle — assistants on phones, buyers checking their invitations, a cluster of journalists comparing notes near the bar. No one looked at Jane twice. She was exactly what she appeared to be: the designer's partner, dressed appropriately, making her way to the car. Invisible in her compliance.

The car was waiting at the curb, a black Mercedes with a driver who held the door open without meeting her eyes. Jane slid in. Adèle followed. The door closed, and Paris began to move past the windows — the Avenue Montaigne with its polished storefronts, the Seine glinting copper in the low sun, the façades of Haussmann buildings catching the light like old gold.

The city was oblivious. Paris did not know that in three hours, a fashion show designed to cement the legacy of one of the most powerful men in the industry would become something else entirely. A reckoning. An exhumation. A mirror held up to the empty space where a conscience should have been.
Jane's phone buzzed.
She pulled it from her clutch and looked at the screen.
**Bertha:** *I'm in position. The jacket is ready. The server is waiting. See you on the other side.*

Three sentences. Three confirmations. The jacket — the final piece of the collection, the one Edward didn't know Bertha had redesigned, the one that would carry the proof on its lining like a confession stitched in silk. The server — the offsite backup that Grace had configured, the one the police would access the moment the show ended, the one that contained fourteen months of intercepted communications, of contracts with forged signatures, of personnel files annotated in Edward's own hand. Position — Bertha was backstage, embedded in the show's production crew under a name that wasn't hers, wearing a headset that gave her access to the audio feed, close enough to the runway to feel the heat of the lights.
Jane typed back: *See you on the runway.*
She put the phone away. Pressed it flat against the bottom of the clutch, beside the extra lipstick she wouldn't need and the hotel key card she might not use again.
She took a breath.

The oatmeal silk moved with her ribs — shallow, controlled, the kind of breathing that looked calm from the outside. But underneath, the crimson dress pressed against her skin like a promise, and her lungs were deeper than the shell allowed anyone to see.
The zipper was hidden. The crimson was waiting. The world was about to see what Edward Rochester built — and what he built it on.
Three hours. Two garments. One zipper.
The most dangerous outfit in fashion history was about to walk into the most watched show on earth.
And Edward Rochester had no idea.
